name: Desktop PC
tagline: Pre-built vs custom build, specs guide, best deals
intro: Buying a desktop PC in 2026 is exciting — the new AMD Ryzen 9000 series, Intel Core Ultra, and NVIDIA RTX 50-series / AMD RX 9000-series GPUs are all here. Whether you want a pre-built machine or to build your own, the key is matching specs to what you'll actually DO with it. A £500 PC handles office work and web browsing beautifully. A £1,500 PC games at 1440p beautifully. A £3,000+ PC is only needed for 4K gaming or serious professional work. Let's figure out what YOU need.

thingsToConsider.title: CPU (Processor)
thingsToConsider.summary: The brain of your PC. Determines how fast everything runs.
thingsToConsider.detail: Two brands: AMD Ryzen and Intel Core. In 2026, AMD Ryzen 7 9700X and Intel Core Ultra 7 265 are the sweet spot for most people. For gaming, the CPU matters less than the GPU — a mid-range CPU won't bottleneck even high-end graphics cards in most games. For productivity (video editing, 3D rendering), more cores matter: look at Ryzen 9 or Core Ultra 9.
thingsToConsider.recommendation: For gaming: Ryzen 5 9600X or Intel Core Ultra 5 245 (both ~£200-250). For productivity: Ryzen 7 9700X or Core Ultra 7 265 (~£300-350). Don't overspend on CPU if gaming is your priority — put the money into GPU instead.

thingsToConsider.title: GPU (Graphics Card)
thingsToConsider.summary: THE most important component for gaming and creative work.
thingsToConsider.detail: NVIDIA GeForce RTX 50-series and AMD Radeon RX 9000-series are the current generation. For 1080p gaming: RTX 5060 or RX 9070 (~£300-400). For 1440p: RTX 5070 or RX 9070 XT (~£500-600). For 4K: RTX 5080 (~£950). For absolute max: RTX 5090 (~£2,000). VRAM (video memory) matters — 12GB minimum in 2026, 16GB ideal. AMD's RX 9070 XT offers 16GB at great value.
thingsToConsider.recommendation: Best value right now: AMD RX 9070 XT (16GB, ~£550) or NVIDIA RTX 5070 (12GB, ~£550). If you're not gaming, integrated graphics on modern CPUs handle office/web/streaming fine — skip the GPU entirely and save £300+.

thingsToConsider.title: RAM (Memory)
thingsToConsider.summary: How much your PC can do at once. More = more multitasking.
thingsToConsider.detail: DDR5 is the current standard. 16GB is minimum for a modern PC. 32GB is the sweet spot for gaming + multitasking. 64GB only needed for video editing, large datasets, or running virtual machines. Speed matters less than capacity — DDR5-5600 or DDR5-6000 is plenty. RAM prices have risen in 2026 so budget accordingly.
thingsToConsider.recommendation: 32GB DDR5 for most people (~£80-120). 16GB if you're on a tight budget. 64GB only if you know you need it.

thingsToConsider.title: Storage (SSD)
thingsToConsider.summary: Where your files, games, and programs live. SSD speed affects load times.
thingsToConsider.detail: NVMe SSDs are standard — they're 5-10x faster than old SATA SSDs. 1TB is minimum in 2026 (games are 50-150GB each). Gen 4 NVMe is the sweet spot for value — Gen 5 is faster but costs more with minimal real-world difference for most users. You can always add a second drive later.
thingsToConsider.recommendation: 1TB NVMe Gen 4 SSD (~£60-80) as your main drive. Add a 2TB if you have lots of games. Skip Gen 5 unless you do heavy video editing.

thingsToConsider.title: Pre-Built vs Custom Build
thingsToConsider.summary: Buy ready-made or build yourself? Both are valid in 2026.
thingsToConsider.detail: Pre-built: convenient, comes with warranty, tested and ready. Custom: you choose every part, often better value, satisfying to build, easier to upgrade. In 2026, the price gap is smaller than ever — some pre-builts are genuinely good value. Custom builds shine for upgradability and avoiding cheap hidden components (PSU, motherboard). Pre-built is fine if you want convenience.
thingsToConsider.recommendation: If you've never built a PC and just want it working: pre-built from PCSpecialist, CyberPower, or AWD-IT (UK-based, good value). If you want to learn and get the best value: build your own — it's like adult LEGO and YouTube guides make it easy.

thingsToConsider.title: Power Supply (PSU)
thingsToConsider.summary: Powers everything. A cheap PSU can damage components or cause instability.
thingsToConsider.detail: 80+ Bronze efficiency minimum. 80+ Gold recommended. Wattage depends on GPU: RTX 5060 needs 550W, RTX 5070 needs 650W, RTX 5080 needs 850W. Always get 100-150W more than the minimum to allow headroom and future upgrades. In pre-builts, this is where manufacturers often cut corners — check what PSU is included.
thingsToConsider.recommendation: 650W 80+ Gold for mid-range builds (~£70). 850W for high-end. Brands to trust: Corsair, Seasonic, EVGA, be quiet!

thingsToConsider.title: Monitor
thingsToConsider.summary: No point having a powerful PC if your monitor can't show the difference.
thingsToConsider.detail: 1080p 144Hz: budget gaming, perfectly good for esports. 1440p 144-165Hz: the sweet spot in 2026 — sharp and smooth. 4K 144Hz: top-end, needs a powerful GPU. Panel types: IPS (best colours, wide viewing angles), VA (deep blacks, good contrast), OLED (best of everything but expensive). Response time: 1ms for competitive gaming, 4-5ms fine for everything else.
thingsToConsider.recommendation: 1440p 144Hz IPS monitor is the 2026 sweet spot (~£200-300). Pair with your GPU — don't buy a 4K monitor with a mid-range GPU.

thingsToConsider.title: Case
thingsToConsider.summary: Houses everything. Affects airflow, noise, looks, and build ease.
thingsToConsider.detail: ATX Mid-Tower is standard — fits most motherboards and GPUs. Look for good airflow (mesh front panel), cable management, and enough fan mounts. RGB is personal preference. Some popular cases: NZXT H5 Flow, Corsair 4000D Airflow, Fractal North, Lian Li Lancool 216.
thingsToConsider.recommendation: Spend £60-100 on a case with a mesh front and good reviews. It'll last multiple builds.

thingsToConsider.title: Operating System
thingsToConsider.summary: Windows 11 is standard for gaming. Linux is free but has game compatibility issues.
thingsToConsider.detail: Windows 11 Home: ~£100-120 retail. Often included in pre-builts. Windows 11 Pro: adds BitLocker, Hyper-V, Remote Desktop — useful for work. You can install Windows 11 without activating it (free, with a watermark and limited customisation). Many OEM keys available for £15-30.
thingsToConsider.recommendation: Windows 11 Home for most people. If you just browse and do office work, consider a Chromebox or Linux.

jargon.meaning: Central Processing Unit — the brain. Handles all calculations. AMD Ryzen and Intel Core are the two brands.
Graphics Processing Unit — the graphics card. Renders images, essential for gaming and video editing. NVIDIA GeForce and AMD Radeon are the two brands.
Random Access Memory — temporary fast storage for running programs. More RAM = more multitasking. DDR5 is the current standard.
Solid State Drive — where your files are stored permanently. NVMe is the fastest type, connecting directly to the motherboard. No moving parts = fast and silent.
Video RAM — memory on the graphics card. More VRAM = higher resolution textures and better performance at 4K. 12-16GB is ideal in 2026.
Power Supply Unit — converts mains electricity to power your components. Rated in watts. 80+ Gold means efficient.
The main circuit board everything plugs into. Must be compatible with your CPU (AMD needs AM5 socket, Intel needs LGA 1851).
Motherboard and case sizes. ATX is standard/full-size. mATX is smaller. ITX is compact. Must match — ATX board won't fit an ITX case.
Screen resolution. 1080p = 1920×1080 (Full HD). 1440p = 2560×1440 (QHD). 4K = 3840×2160. Higher = sharper but needs more GPU power.
How many images your GPU renders per second. 60 FPS is smooth. 144+ FPS is very smooth for competitive gaming.
When one component limits another. E.g. a weak CPU holding back a powerful GPU. Balance your components to avoid this.
AI upscaling technology. NVIDIA DLSS and AMD FSR render at lower resolution then upscale using AI — massive FPS boost with minimal quality loss.
Realistic lighting/reflections in games. Looks stunning but demanding. RTX 50-series handles it much better than older cards.
The slot on the motherboard where NVMe SSDs plug in. Most boards have 2-4 M.2 slots.
A closed-loop liquid cooler for the CPU. Better cooling and quieter than air coolers, but more expensive. 240mm or 360mm radiator sizes.
A compound applied between CPU and cooler to improve heat transfer. Most coolers come with it pre-applied.
Intel XMP and AMD EXPO are one-click settings in BIOS that run your RAM at its rated speed. Without enabling this, RAM often runs slower than advertised.

questions.question: What will you mainly use this PC for?
What resolution do you want to game at?

options.label: Not gaming
options.desc: Skip this — I won't be gaming

questions.question: How much multitasking do you do?
How much storage do you need?
What's your budget for the PC itself? (no monitor/peripherals)

options.label: Under £500
options.desc: Office/web PC or very basic gaming

products.name: Office & Web Build
features: Can play light games (Minecraft, older titles)
Upgradeable — add a GPU later if needed
products.bestFor: Perfect if you just need a fast, reliable PC for work, web browsing, and streaming. Fanstically good value.

products.name: Entry Gaming Build
features: Handles all modern games at medium-high settings
Good for streaming, office, light creative work
missing: 1440p gaming is possible but not maxed
May need RAM upgrade for heavy creative work
products.bestFor: Great first gaming PC or solid all-rounder. Plays everything at 1080p and handles 1440p in many games.

products.name: 1440p Sweet Spot Build
features: Great for video editing and creative work
missing: No Wi-Fi unless motherboard has it
products.bestFor: THE recommended build for 2026. Best value for 1440p gaming. Handles creative work brilliantly. The GPU choice here is the best bang-for-buck available.

products.name: High-End 1440p/4K Build
features: 1440p at ultra settings with ray tracing
Excellent for professional video editing and 3D
missing: Expensive — diminishing returns vs sweet spot
Needs a 4K or high-refresh 1440p monitor to benefit
products.bestFor: For enthusiasts who want near-maximum performance. Great for 4K gaming and professional creative work.

products.name: Ultimate 4K Build
features: 4K gaming at ultra settings with ray tracing
64GB RAM for anything you throw at it
Gen 5 SSD for fastest possible transfers
missing: Only worth it for 4K ultra enthusiasts or professionals
products.bestFor: Money-no-object build. Maximum performance in every scenario. Only buy this if you genuinely need it or just want the best.

products.name: CyberPower Ultra 5 Gaming PC
features: Ready to go out of the box
products.bestFor: Great value pre-built for people who don't want to build. Good specs, UK warranty, ready to play.

products.name: PCSpecialist Vortex GR
features: Named brand parts (Corsair PSU, Samsung SSD)
missing: Some options push the price up quickly
products.bestFor: Best of both worlds — you choose the specs like a custom build, but they build and test it. Highly recommended for people who want quality without DIY.

products.name: Creative Workstation Build
features: 64GB RAM for Premiere, After Effects, Blender
NVIDIA CUDA acceleration for creative apps
Can also game at 1440p when not working
missing: Not the absolute fastest for gaming (GPU is mid-range)
Needs good cooling for sustained workloads
May need additional storage for video archives
products.bestFor: Video editors, 3D artists, music producers. Prioritises CPU cores and RAM over gaming GPU power.

retailers.name: Scan.co.uk
cons: Prices sometimes slightly higher than Amazon
retailers.bestFor: Best all-round UK PC parts retailer. First place to check.

retailers.name: Overclockers UK
retailers.bestFor: Best for GPUs and enthusiast parts. Often has stock when others don't.

retailers.name: Amazon UK
pros: Often cheapest on RAM, SSDs, cases
cons: Check 'Sold by Amazon' — third-party sellers can be dodgy
retailers.bestFor: Best for RAM, SSDs, cases, peripherals. Always price-compare here.

retailers.name: CCL Computers
retailers.bestFor: Often has the best price on CPUs and motherboard bundles.

retailers.name: Ebuyer
pros: Good prices on storage and peripherals
retailers.bestFor: Good for SSDs, monitors, and peripherals.

retailers.name: Currys
retailers.bestFor: Best for monitors, pre-builts, and if you want to see before you buy.

retailers.name: Novatech
retailers.bestFor: Worth checking for competitive pricing, especially on bundles.

retailers.name: PCPartPicker UK
pros: Compares prices across ALL UK retailers
cons: Not a shop — links out to retailers
retailers.bestFor: START HERE. Build your parts list, check compatibility, find the cheapest price for each part.

budget.label: Office / Basic
budget.description: Web browsing, Office, streaming. No gaming GPU needed.

mid.label: 1080p Gaming / All-rounder
